explanation.summary: Fats are carrying this food most, while minerals are the biggest drag. It lands in D tier.
explanation.whyThisTier: This food lands in D tier with an overall score of 17 from a base nutrition score of 17. Context barely changed the result.

topPros.title: makes meals feel more balanced
topPros.explanation: The category has real value beyond single-nutrient stats.

topPros.title: helps add volume without much calorie cost
topPros.explanation: That makes vegetables easier to use often.

topPros.title: good volume for the calories
topPros.explanation: That matters for satiety and general usefulness.

topCons.title: energy density is very low
topCons.explanation: Good for some use cases, but not enough on its own.

topCons.title: taste and texture can limit compliance
topCons.explanation: A strong paper profile still needs real-world repeatability.

topCons.title: rarely carries a meal by itself
topCons.explanation: That is normal here, but still part of the tradeoff.

closing.summary: Onion does have a few real-world upsides. but the nutrition case is still weak and the downsides drag it down hard. best only when you need a low-commitment add-on rather than a nutrient-dense anchor.
closing.useCaseNote: Best only when you need a low-commitment add-on rather than a nutrient-dense anchor.
closing.cta: Would you rank it the same, or nah?

reviewChecklist: Verify nutrient values are sourced well enough for publishable use.
Review narration for category repetition and trim any awkward lines.
Confirm subtitle density stays readable at phone speed.
Replace placeholder hero/sprite art with final asset selection.
Check that the final tier verdict feels editorially defensible.
nextHumanStep: Review the generated narration + manifest, then use the manifest as the handoff into visual assembly / editing.

scenes.narrationText: 0.1g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, clean nutrient density is the real test.
scenes.subtitleText: 0.1g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, clean nutrient density is the real test.

scenes.narrationText: 9.3g of carbs. the sugar load matters more than you would want. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.
scenes.subtitleText: 9.3g of carbs. the sugar load matters more than you would want. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.

scenes.narrationText: 1.1g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.
